evals.name: framework-selection-summarization
evals.prompt: I'm building a summarization eval for long Wikipedia articles. Which discourse framework should I use?
evals.expected_output: Should: (1) recommend RST — it models hierarchical nucleus/satellite which directly maps to summarization (which units to keep, which to compress); (2) note RST-DT as English training; (3) compare with PDTB (local relations, less suitable for summarization-coherence); (4) mention GUM if multi-layer eval needed; (5) flag per-language RST coverage gaps; (6) Stede textbook as canonical reference.

evals.name: pro-drop-zero-anaphora-mandarin
evals.prompt: I'm running coreference resolution on Mandarin Wikipedia using a model trained on OntoNotes English. The pronoun chains look incomplete. Bug?
evals.expected_output: Should: (1) NOT a bug per se — Mandarin is pro-drop; 30-40% of pronouns are dropped; (2) English-trained coref doesn't model zero anaphora; chains silently miss the dropped pronouns; (3) recommend wl-coref with zero-anaphora extension OR a Mandarin-specific coref model (CRAC participants); (4) generalize to Japanese, Korean, Spanish, Italian — all pro-drop; (5) for RAG / entity extraction in pro-drop, don't trust English-coref output without explicit zero-anaphora handling.

evals.name: rag-citation-faithfulness
evals.prompt: Our RAG system passes a 'citation overlap' check (cited source contains the claimed phrase). But users complain about wrong citations. What's missing?
evals.expected_output: Should: (1) citation overlap is INCOMPLETE — checks (a) phrase appears in source but misses (b) the claim coreferentially matches what the user asked; (2) recommend NLI for claim-source entailment + coref check for question-answer alignment; (3) note this is a discourse-coherence problem disguised as a retrieval problem; (4) suggest concrete probe construction for citation-faithfulness eval.
